title: Source-Grounded AI Wikis
subtitle: An AI wiki is useful when every explanation remains tied to the material that produced it.
summary: Lykke turns Canvas course content into generated wiki pages, flashcards, quizzes, and study artifacts.

artifact.title: A page that regenerates with its sources
artifact.description: A source-grounded wiki has definition blocks, examples, pitfalls, and study aids that can be regenerated when the course material changes.

sections.heading: The Core Object
body: A source-grounded AI wiki page is not just a generated essay. It is a structured teaching object built from specific source blocks.
The useful unit is the claim: a definition, theorem, step, example, warning, or formula that can be traced back to a source.
sections.callout: The wiki should make the student smarter without asking them to trust unsupported generated prose.

sections.heading: Page Anatomy
body: A strong page starts with the concept, then gives mechanics, examples, variations, common mistakes, and a study check.
Interactive or visual moments matter because many technical ideas are spatial, procedural, or comparative.

rows: Show how it works step by step
Move from abstract rule to concrete use
Show how learners usually get it wrong

pitfalls: Writing generic study-wrapper prose instead of directly teaching the topic.
Generating citations that do not exist.
Making diagrams decorative instead of explanatory.
Not versioning regenerated pages when source material changes.

quiz.question: What makes an AI wiki source-grounded?
options: It has every claim tied to source material
quiz.explanation: Grounding comes from traceable source support, not from length or style.
